coredata.dc:title: Graphene transparent conductive electrodes doped with graphene quantum dots-mixed silver nanowires for highly-flexible organic solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Shin, Dong Hee
Seo, Sang Woo
Kim, Jong Min
Lee, Ha Seung
Choi, Suk-Ho
coredata.dc:description: Recent active studies on flexible photovoltaic cells strongly call for matchable flexible transparent electrodes. Graphene (GR) is one of the promising candidates as transparent conductive electrodes (TCEs) for flexible photovoltaic cells, but high sheet resistance of GR limits the efficiency of the cells. Here, we first fabricate GR TCEs doped with graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-mixed silver nanowires (Ag NWs) on polyethylene terephthalate substrates for highly-flexible organic solar cells (OSCs). With increasing doping concentration of GQDs to 0.03 g/L, the sheet resistance of the Ag NWs/GR TCE decreases to ∼92 Ω/sq whilst its work function increases to ∼4.53 eV, resulting in 3.66% power-conversion efficiency (PCE). In addition, the GQDs enhance the bending flexibility of the Ag NWs/GR TCEs, thereby maintaining the initial PCE of the OSCs over 90% even after 1000 bending cycles at a curvature radius of 4 mm.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
Silver nanowires
Graphene quantum dots
Flexibility
Organic solar cell

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FE-SEM image and (b) EDX spectrum of GQDs-mixed Ag NWs/GR TCE at nG = 0.02 g/L. The inset in (a) shows a real image of the TCE. (c) Optical transmittance spectra, (d) sheet resistances, and work functions of GR, Ag NWs/GR, and GQDs-mixed Ag NWs/GR TCEs. Here, nG is varied from 0.01 to 0.03 g/L. The yellow brackets in the inset of (c) show real images of the Ag NWs/GR TCEs with/without GQDs, indicating their excellent transparency. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Normalized sheet resistance (ΔRs/Rs) of Ag NWs/GR TCEs as functions of curvature radius for various nG under (a) inner bending and (b) outer bending.

(a) Normalized ΔRs/Rs of Ag NWs/GR TCEs as functions of bending cycle at a curvature radius of 4 mm for various nG. FE-SEM plan-view images of Ag NWs/GR/PETs before and after 1000 bending cycles: (b) and (c) without GQDs, (d) and (e) with GQDs (nG = 0.02 g/L). The bending tests were done at a 0.5 Hz bending frequency under outer bending. The scale bars all indicate 500 nm.

(a) Schematic structure and (b) energy band diagram of a typical FOSC containing Ag NWs/GR TCEs with/without GQDs. The inset in (a) shows a real image of the FOSC and the blue arrow in (b) indicates the shift of Fermi level in the Ag NWs/GR TCEs. (c) Photo J-V curves, (d) EQE spectra, and integrated Jsc of the Ag NWs/GR TCE/FOSCs for various nG. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Normalized PCEs of the Ag NWs/GR TCE/FOSCs as functions of bending radius for nG = 0 and 0.02 g/L. The inset shows real images of the FOSCs at each R. (b) Normalized PCEs of the Ag NWs/GR TCE/FOSCs as functions of bending cycle at a fixed bending radius of 4 mm for nG = 0 and 0.02 g/L. Here, the data are presented with error bars of standard deviation after being averaged for four separate FOSCs.
Photovoltaic parameters of flexible organic solar cells with varying nG.

head.ce:title: Graphene transparent conductive electrodes doped with graphene quantum dots-mixed silver nanowires for highly-flexible organic solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Physics and Institute of Natural Sciences, Kyung Hee University, Yongin 17104, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Applied Physics and Institute of Natural Sciences

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Two authors have contributed equally to this study.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recent active studies on flexible photovoltaic cells strongly call for matchable flexible transparent electrodes. Graphene (GR) is one of the promising candidates as transparent conductive electrodes (TCEs) for flexible photovoltaic cells, but high sheet resistance of GR limits the efficiency of the cells. Here, we first fabricate GR TCEs doped with graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-mixed silver nanowires (Ag NWs) on polyethylene terephthalate substrates for highly-flexible organic solar cells (OSCs). With increasing doping concentration of GQDs to 0.03 g/L, the sheet resistance of the Ag NWs/GR TCE decreases to ∼92 Ω/sq whilst its work function increases to ∼4.53 eV, resulting in 3.66% power-conversion efficiency (PCE). In addition, the GQDs enhance the bending flexibility of the Ag NWs/GR TCEs, thereby maintaining the initial PCE of the OSCs over 90% even after 1000 bending cycles at a curvature radius of 4 mm.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past two decades, flexible organic solar cells (FOSCs) have received considerable attention due to their low cost, highly-scalable roll-to-roll preparation, light weight, and excellent mechanical flexibility [1–9]. One of the major issues in the studies of FOSCs is how to maintain their performance even after repeated bending tests at various curvature radius (R) [10–13]. For achieving this purpose, the electrode as well as the photoactive medium is required to be highly flexible with their excellency in conductivity and transparency. Graphene (GR) has been proposed as a promising material for transparent conductive electrodes (TCEs) of FOSCs due to its mechanical/chemical robustness, excellent electrical and optical properties, and potentially low-cost fabrication processes [14–16]. Several studies have demonstrated the feasibility of GR-TCE-based optoelectronic devices [17,18], including organic solar cells (OSCs) [19,20], but high sheet resistance (Rs) of bare GR cannot meet the requirement for high-efficiency OSCs.

Various methods, such as chemical doping [21–23] and formation of metal nanoparticles or metal nanowires (NWs) [24–26], have been developed to reduce Rs of GR sheets, Among them, Ag NWs are attractive as a dopant for GR, especially in GR-based solar cells, because Ag NWs reduce the reflectance as well as enhance the absorption [25–27]. Recently, the performance of the FOSCs was also improved by employing Ag NWs/GR TCEs [20], whose Rs increased by repeated bending tests [28,29], resulting in the deterioration of the power-conversion efficiency (PCE). Very recently, hybrid TCEs made of Ag NWs-GR oxide composite materials [30] were employed for Si-based solar cells, but not for flexible devices. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been regarded as a rising material in optoelectronic applications due to their tunable band gap, low toxicity, environmental compatibility, and chemical inertness [31–33]. With GQDs, it may be possible to increase the ultra violet-visible absorption and thus further increase the PCE of Ag NWs/GR TCE-based FOSCs. In this work, we employ Ag NWs mixed with GQDs as a dopant to reduce Rs of GR TCEs and enhance their flexibility, thereby improving the performance of the FOSCs. The FOSCs exhibit a maximum PCE of 3.66% at a GQD concentration (nG) = 0.02 g/L. In addition, the FOSCs become much more flexible by containing GQDs in the Ag NWs/GR TCEs, as demonstrated by bending tests at various R. We also analyze the photovoltaic parameters based on the structural, electrical, and optical properties of the Ag NWs/GR TCEs with/without GQDs.

Single-layer GR layers were grown on Cu foils by chemical vapor deposition, and transferred onto polyethylene terephthalate (PET) substrates in the same manner as previously reported [34]. Ag NWs powder was dissolved in isopropyl alcohol to prepare a 0.1 wt% Ag NWs solution. The Ag NWs solution was coated on the GR/PETs at 1500 rpm for 1 min, and then dried on a hot plate at 100 °C for 2 min. GQDs solution for nG = 0.01–0.03 g/L was subsequently spin-coated on the Ag NWs/GR/PETs at 4000 rpm for 1min, and air-dried at room temperature. To prepare the FOSCs, PEDOT:PSS was spin-coated on the Ag NWs/GR films with/without GQDs at 3000 rpm for 60s, and subsequently annealed at 130 °C for 15 min in air. This process was repeated two times to obtain continuously-smooth film. Then, a blend solution of 25-mg P3HT and 25-mg PCBM in 1-ml 1,2-dichlorobenzene was spin-coated onto the PEDOT:PSS layers at 700 rpm for 60 s under a nitrogen atmosphere. The devices were then treated by solvent annealing at 120 °C for 2 h, and further annealed at 110 °C for 10 min. Finally, Al electrodes were deposited on the devices using a thermal evaporator at a pressure of 10−6 Torr.

The transmittance, sheet resistance, and work function of GR were measured by ultraviolet–visible-near IR optical spectroscopy (Varian, Cary 5000), 4 probe van der Pauw method (Dasol eng, model FPP-HS8-40K), and Kevin probe force microscopy (Park systems, model XE100), respectively. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured at room temperature using a 325 nm line of the HeCd laser as the excitation source. The morphologies of GQDs were analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Surface morphology of the doped GR was analyzed by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) equipped with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) system. The surface of the PEDOT:PSS on TCEs were measured by atomic force microscopy (AFM). The current density-voltage (J–V) characteristics of the FOSCs were measured using a solar simulator under illumination of 1 Sun (100 mWcm−2 AM 1.5G). External quantum efficiency (EQE) was measured under short circuit conditions while the cells were illuminated by a light source system with monochromator. The illumination area was fixed to 4.52 mm2.

The FE-SEM image in Fig. 1 a shows hybrid formation of Ag NWs and GQDs on a GR sheet, and the inset image demonstrates high flexibility/transparency of the hybrid TCE (For nG-dependent SEM images, see Supporting Information Figs. S1a–c). The diameter of the GQDs was measured to be about 7 nm by using high-magnification TEM (Supporting Information Fig. S1d). The optical absorption and PL spectra of the GQDs aqueous solution are peaked at 356 and 450 nm, respectively, (Supporting Information Fig. S1e), as can be expected from previous reports [35,36]. The well formation of the Ag NWs on the GR surface was further confirmed by EDX analysis. Fig. 1b shows a clear Ag peak near 3 keV in the EDX spectrum. Fig. 1c compares optical transmittance spectra of GR and Ag NWs/GR without/with GQDs on PET substrates for various nG. The transmittance of the Ag NWs/GR TCEs with GQDs is smaller than that of bare GR TCE or the Ag NWs/GR TCE without GQDs, but is still near 90% in the visible range. The Ag NWs/GR TCEs with GQDs have a specific absorption peak at around 350 nm, as shown in Fig. 1c, resulting from the GQDs [32,33], also demonstrating well formation of the GQDs on the Ag NWs/GR TCEs. The yellow brackets in the inset indicate the excellent transmittance of the Ag NWs/GR TCEs, irrespective of the existence of the GQDs. The Rs of the bare GR is ∼780 ± 50 Ω cm−2, decreases to 123 ± 8 Ω cm−2 by doping with Ag NWs, and is further lowered to 92 ± 6 Ω cm−2 by additional coating of GQDs up to nG = 0.03 g/L, as shown in Fig. 1d. It is believed that the GQDs significantly reduces the contact resistance between the Ag NWs, thereby enhancing the conductivity of the Ag NWs network. The work functions of the bare GR TCE and Ag NWs/GR TCE without GQDs are 4.55 ± 0.04 and 4.42 ± 0.02 eV, respectively, as shown in Fig. 1d. In other words, the Ag NWs tend to donate electrons to GR, thereby making GR n-type, as reported before [34,35]. On the contrary, the work function of the Ag NWs/GR TCE increases from 4.42 ± 0.02 to 4.53 ± 0.02 eV as nG increases to 0.03 g/L.

The mechanical stabilities of the Ag NWs-doped GR TCEs without/with GQDs on PET substrates were checked by inner and outer bending tests at 0.5 Hz bending frequency for various R of ∞, 8, 6, 4, and 2 mm, as shown in Fig. 2 . The inner/outer bending induces compressive/tensile stress on the GR films, respectively. Under the compressive bending, the Ag NWs/GR TCEs show excellent mechanical stabilities in Rs, irrespective of the inclusion of the GQDs, as shown in Fig. 2a. Under tensile bending, the Rs of the Ag NWs/GR TCEs without GQDs increases significantly with decreasing R, as shown in Fig. 2b. In contrast, the R-dependent change of the Rs is remarkably reduced for the TCEs with GQDs by increasing nG to 0.02 g/L, but for nG ≥ 0.02, it is almost saturated. These results suggest that the GQDs relieve the damage of the Ag NWs caused by the networking. The nG-dependent evolution of the relative sheet resistance (ΔRs/Rs) at a fixed R of 4 mm shows similar behaviors, as shown in Fig. 3 a. So, further characterizations were done at nG = 0.02 g/L. Fig. 3b–e shows plan-view FE-SEM images of the two TCEs without/with GQDs (nG = 0.02 g/L) before and after the bending tests. The cracks are clearly observed only in the Ag NWs without GQDs on the GR surface after the bending tests, as shown in Fig. 3c, indicating that the bending-induced sharp increase of ΔRs/Rs can be mainly attributed to the cracking of the Ag NWs. Therefore, we believe that the GQDs protect the Ag NWs as well as prevent them from being apart from each other, consistent with previous reports on carbon-based-materials-mixed-Ag NWs [25,37,38].

To check the bending stabilities of bare GR without Ag NWs, we measured Raman spectrum and Rs after bending tests for bare GR/PET at R = 4 mm. Almost no change in Rs was found even after 1000 bending cycles (Supporting Information Fig. S2a). Raman G and 2D bands should be observed in bare GR, but the G band is overlapped with that of PET (Supporting Information Fig. S2b). Therefore, the variation of the 2D band was monitored during the bending tests for bare GR/PET. The 2D band was almost invariant after 1000 bending cycles, indicating excellent stretchability of the bare GR/PET.

Fig. 4 a shows a schematic of a typical FOSC consisting of PET/GR TCE/PEDOT:PSS/P3HT:PCBM/Al. The wettability of the PEDOT:PSS on Ag NWs/GR without/with GQDs was checked by AFM before subsequent processes for completing the FOSCs. The AFM images show similar roughnesses (Rq) of the PEDOT:PSS on Ag NWs/GR, irrespective of the inclusion of the GQDs (Supporting Information Fig. S3), indicating well formation of the PEDOT:PSS layer on the Ag NWs/GR surface. Together with well-known band energies of PEDOT:PSS, P3HT:PCBM, and Al, the measured work functions of the GR TCEs, as shown in Fig. 1d, were used for describing the band structure of the FOSCs, as shown in Fig. 4b. All the photovoltaic parameters such as open-circuit voltage (Voc), short-circuit current density (Jsc), fill factor (FF), and PCE, extracted from the J-V curves in Fig. 4c and summarized in Table 1 , are improved with increasing nG to 0.02 g/L, but remain almost unchanged for nG ≥ 0.02.

To further understand the effect of the GQDs on Jsc, EQE spectra were measured with increasing nG. As shown in Fig. 4d, the EQE of the FOSCs is enhanced over full range of wavelength with increasing nG to 0.02 g/L, but for nG ≥ 0.02, it is almost saturated. The Ag NWs/GR TCEs with GQDs have smaller Rs and lower energy barrier at the interface with the PEDOT: PSS layer, resulting from larger work function, as shown in Fig. 1d, thereby showing better photovoltaic properties. These results are consistent with the fact that the energy barrier at the interface with GR and FF (and Jsc) critically depend on the work function and Rs of the TCEs, respectively [39,40]. The nG-dependent behavior of the EQE is consistent with that of Jsc, as shown in Table 1, resulting from the strong correlation of Jsc with integrated EQE [41]. These behaviors are almost unchanged even though the photovoltaic parameters are averaged for 16 FOSCs (Supporting Information Fig. S4).

We also studied long-term stabilities of the FOSCs by measuring the PCE for 10 days (Supporting Information Fig. S5). The environmental temperature and humidity for the measurements were typically 25 °C and 40%, respectively. The FOSCs with/without GQDs devices lost 6 and 14% of their original PCEs, respectively after 10 days, indicating much better durability of the former one. These results suggest that the long-term stabilities were greatly enhanced by adding GQDs as a protective layer for the Ag NWs TCEs, consistent with the previous reports on carbon base-Ag NWs TCEs [25,37,38].

To check the practical availability of the FOSCs for a flexible power source, their operational stabilities against repeated bending tests at various R were evaluated. The PCEs of the FOSCs with/without GQDs after bending tests at R = ∞, 8, 6, 4, and 2 mm are shown in Fig. 5 a. The PCEs of FOSCs without GQDs were deteriorated down to 98.8, 97.8, 95.2, and 92.8% of their original values at R = 8, 6, 4, and 2 mm, respectively, far below the minimum criterion for the flexible photovoltaic applications. In contrast, the PCEs of the FOSCs with GQDs were maintained at ∼98% of their original values for R = 8, 6, 4, and 2 mm. The evolution of the PCE throughout 1000 bending cycles at R = 4 mm was also investigated for the FOSCs, as shown in Fig. 5b. The PCE of the FOSCs without GQDs is severely degraded to 42, 50, and 60% of its original value after 500, 750, and 1000 bending cycles, respectively at R = 4 mm. In contrast, there was only ∼8% reduction in the PCE of the FOSCs with GQDs after 1000 bending cycles. These behaviors are very much consistent with those of ΔRs/Rs, as shown in Fig. 3a, and very promising for the application of the FOSCs as an effectively-flexible power source for advanced portable and wearable electronic/photonic appliances.

High-performance FOSCs were successfully fabricated by employing GQDs-mixed Ag NWs/GR TCEs. The Ag NWs/GR/PET substrates exhibited high flexibility by containing the GQDs. In contrast, the Ag NWs/GR/PET substrates without GQDs showed significant degradation of Rs during bending tests due to the break-off or damage of the Ag NWs. Similarly, the FOSCs constructed on the GQDs-mixed Ag NWs/GR TCE/PET substrates exhibited excellent bending stabilities, maintaining their original 3.66% PCE at nG = 0.02 g/L over 90% even after 1000 bending cycles at R = 4 mm. In contrast, the PCE of the FOSCs without GQDs significantly decreased to 60% of its initial value after the same bending tests. In addition, The FOSCs with/without GQDs lost 6 and 14% of their initial PCEs after kept at 25 °C and 40% humidity for 10 days. These results were attributed to the role of the GQDs as a protecting layer for Ag NWs/GR TCEs. Our findings point out how important flexible electrodes are in flexible solar cells.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT & Future Planning (NRF-2017R1A2B3006054).
